message:
test(core): property-based tests for process triage invariants (wa-mkdn)

25 proptest properties covering TriageCategory ordering/priority/auto_safe/
protected/display/serde, TriageAction display/serde for all variants,
ClassifiedProcess serde, TriageConfig serde/defaults, classify() for
system processes/zombies/stuck tests/stuck CLIs/build tools/dev servers/
agents, and TriagePlan sorting/filtering invariants.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"wa-bsf9","title":"Extension sandboxing: WASM or Lua runtime for safe custom code execution","description":"
# Extension Sandboxing

## Purpose
Run custom extension code safely without risking system security.

## Options Evaluated

### 1. WASM (Recommended)
- Pros: Strong isolation, multi-language, proven
- Cons: Larger binary, more complex
- Libraries: wasmtime, wasmer

### 2. Lua
- Pros: Simple, fast, battle-tested (Neovim/WezTerm)
- Cons: Single language, needs careful sandboxing
- Libraries: rlua, mlua

## Recommendation: WASM via wasmtime
- Better isolation guarantees
- Supports Rust, Go, C, AssemblyScript
- WASI for controlled system access

## Sandbox Capabilities
```rust
pub struct ExtensionSandbox {
    engine: wasmtime::Engine,
    linker: wasmtime::Linker<SandboxState>,
    capabilities: SandboxCapabilities,
}

pub struct SandboxCapabilities {
    pub read_pane_output: bool,
    pub send_notifications: bool,
    pub http_requests: bool,
    pub file_access: FileAccessScope,
}
```

## Capability Levels
- Level 1: Read-only (patterns only)
- Level 2: Read + notify (most workflows)
- Level 3: Read + notify + HTTP (integrations)
- Level 4: Full (admin extensions)

## Extension Manifest
```toml
[extension]
name = "my-patterns"
capabilities = ["read_pane_output"]
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] WASM runtime integrated
- [ ] Capability-based permissions
- [ ] Extensions cannot access system without permission
- [ ] Untrusted extension cannot crash wa

## Testing
- Unit tests for sandbox policy enforcement and resource limits.
- Integration tests running a restricted extension in a sandbox.
- Security checks: verify no filesystem/network escape.

 {"id":"wa-bv81","title":"E2E script: workspace isolation (no cross-project DB leakage; --workspace honored)","description":"# Task: E2E script — workspace isolation

## Goal
Prove that wa honors workspace scoping and does not leak data across projects.

This is a critical UX property:
- users run multiple projects in parallel
- agents should not read/write the wrong DB
- debugging depends on knowing which workspace you are looking at

## Scenario
- Spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token: WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Steps
1) Start wa watch with --workspace <A>.
2) Wait until WORKSPACE_TOKEN is persisted (wa robot search --workspace <A>).
3) Run wa robot search --workspace <B> for WORKSPACE_TOKEN.

Assertions
- In workspace A: the token is found.
- In workspace B: the token is NOT found.

Optional extension
- Start wa watch in workspace B and ensure it creates a distinct DB.

## Artifacts
- effective config for both workspaces
- wa watch logs
- wa robot search outputs for A and B

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.
- Ensure `./scripts/e2e_test.sh --all` includes this case (or mark non-default with justification).

## Logging
- Follow the standard E2E harness logging contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- Emit per-step start/end markers with timestamps + durations.
- Include case name and pane ids in every log line.
- Redact secrets (device codes, tokens) in logs and artifacts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Test is deterministic.
- Failures make it obvious whether the bug is:
  - workspace resolution
  - DB path derivation
  - CLI flag propagation


## Testing
- Meta-validation:
  - Assert the two workspaces produce distinct DB/log paths (not just search behavior).
  - Add a control assertion that workspace B can find a different token once it has ingested something.

- Artifact validation:
  - Artifacts must include the derived paths (workspace root, db path, log path) to make mis-scoping diagnosable.

 {"id":"wa-c5dn","title":"Implement write batching + WAL checkpoint/optimize strategy","description":"# Task: Write batching + checkpoint/optimize strategy

## Goal
Reduce write amplification and keep WAL/FTS healthy under sustained ingest.

## Requirements
- Writer batching:
  - batch segment inserts and index updates
  - bounded batch size (time/rows/bytes)
- WAL strategy:
  - periodic checkpoints based on size/time
  - avoid long stalls (do work incrementally)
- FTS maintenance:
  - decide when to run optimize/vacuum-like operations
  - ensure maintenance is explicit and does not surprise users

## Testing
- Integration tests:
  - simulate sustained ingest and assert:
    - WAL does not grow without bound
    - queries remain available
- Benchmarks:
  - compare before/after ingest throughput

## Acceptance Criteria
- Sustained ingest remains stable and storage maintenance is predictable.

 {"id":"wa-c7o","title":"[EPIC] Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation","description":"# Phase 2: Workflow Engine & Automation

## Overview
This epic builds the workflow automation system that responds to detected patterns. The workflow engine is what makes wa "active" rather than just a passive monitor - it can take corrective actions when agents hit limits, experience compaction, need authentication, etc.

## Strategic Importance
The workflow system transforms wa from a "monitoring tool" into an "automation platform". This is where the real value emerges:
- Automatic context recovery after compaction
- Automatic account rotation on usage limits
- Automatic authentication renewal
- Foundation for more sophisticated multi-agent coordination

## Key Components

### 1. Workflow Engine Architecture
- Trait-based workflow definition: \`Workflow\` trait with name, description, handles, steps, execute_step
- Step results: Continue, Done, Retry, Abort, WaitFor
- WaitCondition: Pattern, PaneIdle, External
- Per-pane workflow locking (prevent conflicting workflows)
- Idempotent, recoverable, audited execution

### 2. Handle Usage Limits Workflow (Codex First)
Steps:
1. Exit current session gracefully (Ctrl-C twice for Codex)
2. Parse session info for resume (session ID, token usage)
3. Select next available account
4. Perform account authentication
5. Resume previous session
6. Send continue prompt

This is the most complex workflow and proves out the architecture.

### 3. Handle Compaction Workflow
Steps:
1. Wait for compaction to complete (stabilization window)
2. Send agent-specific context refresh prompt
   - Claude Code: "Reread AGENTS.md so it's still fresh in your mind."
   - Codex: "Please re-read AGENTS.md and any key project context files."
   - Gemini: "Please re-examine AGENTS.md and project context."

### 4. Browser Automation Skeleton
- Playwright integration structure
- Browser profile persistence
- OpenAI device auth flow (skeleton)
- Anthropic OAuth flow (skeleton)
- Google auth flow (skeleton)
- Semi-automated fallback (open browser for human when automation fails)

### 5. CAUT Integration
- Shell out to \`caut\` for usage tracking
- get_usage(service) - current usage for OpenAI/Anthropic/Google
- refresh_usage(service) - refresh from APIs
- Account selection logic based on percent_remaining

## Workflow Design Constraints
Every workflow must be:
- **Idempotent**: Re-running doesn't make a mess
- **Recoverable**: Can resume after a crash
- **Audited**: Every step recorded (what was observed, what was sent)
- **Guarded**: Each step validates state before action

## Workflow Execution Model
Each step follows: Observe → Decide → Act → Verify

Step can return:
- \`Continue\` - proceed to next step
- \`WaitFor(condition)\` - pause until condition met
- \`Retry(after)\` - retry same step after delay
- \`Abort(reason)\` - stop workflow with error
- \`Done(result)\` - workflow completed successfully

## Data-Driven Workflow Descriptors
In addition to compiled Rust workflows, support YAML/TOML descriptors for simple prompt injection sequences:

\`\`\`yaml
# ~/.config/wa/workflows/custom_compaction.yaml
name: custom_compaction_prompt
trigger:
  event: session.compaction
  agent: claude_code
steps:
  - wait_stable: 2s
  - send: |
      Please re-read AGENTS.md and the project's README.md.
  - wait_for: "❯"
\`\`\`

## Success Criteria
- [ ] Workflow engine can execute multi-step workflows
- [ ] handle_compaction workflow works end-to-end for Claude Code
- [ ] handle_usage_limits workflow works end-to-end for Codex (at least skeleton)
- [ ] Browser automation structure is in place (can be extended in Phase 3)
- [ ] CAUT integration returns account usage data
- [ ] Workflows are audited and recoverable

## Dependencies
- Depends on Phase 1 (Foundation) - needs pattern engine, storage, watcher

## Technical Notes

### Why Codex First for Usage Limits?
- Codex has the clearest device auth flow
- Resume mechanism is well-documented
- Good test case for the complexity

### Semi-Automated Browser Auth
If Playwright can't complete auth (MFA, CAPTCHA, password):
1. Open non-headless browser
2. Request human to complete auth
3. Persist browser profile for future sessions
4. Notify when auth complete

This provides graceful degradation.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:47:54.89521276Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","closed_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:07.794108825Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of wa-nu4.1 (Phase 2: Workflows). wa-nu4.1 has correct dependency chain. Content merged conceptually."}

## Goal
Make storage behavior predictable and tunable for power users.

## Requirements
- Document:
  - how WAL/checkpointing works in wa
  - how to interpret index lag signals
  - recommended maintenance commands (explicit vacuum/optimize)

 {"id":"wa-v0d3","title":"Docs: secure distributed mode (setup, defaults, rotation, troubleshooting)","description":"# Task: Docs for secure distributed mode

## Goal
Document secure distributed mode so operators can enable it confidently.

## Content outline
- What distributed mode is (agent vs aggregator) and when to use it.
- Safe defaults:
  - local-only bind by default
  - explicit flags required for remote binds
- Security configuration:
  - token auth
  - TLS/mTLS setup (if enabled)
  - allowlist/identity model
- Rotation procedures:
  - how to rotate token/certs
  - how to verify effective config (`wa doctor`)
- Troubleshooting:
  - common failures and stable error codes
  - where to find logs and E2E artifacts

## Acceptance Criteria
- A future operator can deploy secure distributed mode without consulting PLAN.md.
- Docs are explicit about the safety model and how to avoid accidental exposure.
","notes":"Completed by MaroonCreek (2026-02-06): rewrote docs/distributed-security-spec.md into an operator runbook covering compile-time feature gate, safe defaults, token+TLS and token+mtls setup snippets, rotation procedures, doctor verification, runtime security error codes, and artifact/logging guidance. Updated README.md with a Secure Distributed Mode section and corrected limitations text to avoid contradicting distributed support.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T22:16:32.236305101Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.173547-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-06T16:58:55.574151901Z"}

 {"id":"wa-v3k5","title":"CLI: config profile list/create/apply","description":"## What
Implement profile management commands.

## Why
Operators need a simple CLI workflow for switching configurations.

## How
- `wa config profile list`
- `wa config profile create <name> --from current`
- `wa config profile apply <name>`
- Validate profiles before apply

## Success Criteria
- CLI commands operate without modifying unrelated config keys
- Errors are actionable and include diffs on failure","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CobaltGlen","created_at":"2026-02-01T03:06:35.837900509Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.277629-05:00","closed_at":"2026-02-05T09:07:27.227849755Z","close_reason":"Implemented config profile list/create/apply CLI with manifest updates and overlay merge","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-v3k5","depends_on_id":"wa-wo94","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-v3k5","depends_on_id":"wa-ts1a","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-06T04:09:22Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"wa-v40a","title":"E2E script: backup/restore cycle (export, corrupt, import, verify)","description":"# E2E script: backup/restore cycle (export, corrupt, import, verify)

## Goal
Prove end-to-end that backup/restore is safe, user-friendly, and reliable:
- export produces a self-contained, verifiable backup artifact
- import rejects corrupt backups with actionable errors
- replace/merge semantics behave correctly
- pre-import safety backups are created when mutating an existing workspace
- no secrets leak into logs/artifacts

This is the system-level validation for `wa-1wg`.

## Key constraints
- Must follow the standard E2E harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and run via the standard runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`).
- **No destructive shell cleanup** in test scripts (no `rm -rf`).
  - Use fresh temporary workspaces/directories per scenario to reset state.
- Deterministic synchronization:
  - no fixed sleeps as the primary sync primitive
  - wait on explicit conditions (segments/events present, watcher stopped, etc.) with bounded timeouts

## Test setup (harness)
- Use a dedicated E2E workspace root for this case.
- Each scenario uses its own fresh workspace dir:
  - `workspace_a/`, `workspace_b/`, …
- Generate minimal deterministic data:
  - spawn a dummy pane that prints a unique token and/or emits a known event
  - run `wa watch` long enough to persist at least one segment
  - stop watcher cleanly (`wa stop --workspace …` or SIGINT with bounded wait)

## Scenarios

### 1) Clean export/import round-trip
**Intent:** exported backup round-trips core data.

Steps:
1. Populate `workspace_a` with known captured data (at least one segment + one searchable token).
2. Export:
   - `wa backup export --workspace workspace_a --output artifacts/backup.wa`
3. Record a minimal evidence snapshot from `workspace_a` (stable fields only):
   - segment count (or “token is searchable” evidence)
   - event count (if applicable)
4. Import into a fresh `workspace_b`:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_b artifacts/backup.wa --replace --yes`
5. Verify `workspace_b` matches the recorded evidence (stable fields only).

Assertions:
- backup file exists and is verifiable (`--verify` or checksum metadata).
- restored workspace contains the expected token/search hit.

### 2) Corrupt backup rejection
**Intent:** corruption is detected and rejected.

Steps:
1. Create a valid backup.
2. Corrupt it by appending bytes:
   - `printf 'garbage' >> artifacts/backup_corrupt.wa`
3. Import must fail:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_b artifacts/backup_corrupt.wa …` (expected non-zero)

Assertions:
- error mentions integrity verification (checksum/manifest) and provides remediation.
- no partial restore occurs (workspace remains empty or unchanged).

### 3) Pre-import safety backup is created
**Intent:** importing into an existing workspace never destroys data without preserving a recovery artifact.

Steps:
1. Populate `workspace_c` with some data.
2. Export an “external” backup from `workspace_a` (or another workspace).
3. Import into `workspace_c` with replace mode:
   - `wa backup import --workspace workspace_c artifacts/external.wa --replace --yes`

Assertions:
- A pre-import backup file is created in the configured backup destination for `workspace_c`.
- The pre-import backup is itself verifiable.

### 4) Metadata-only backup
**Intent:** metadata-only export exists and is meaningfully smaller.

Steps:
1. Export full backup and metadata-only backup.
2. Compare file sizes.

Assertions:
- metadata-only file is smaller by a meaningful factor (threshold chosen to avoid flakiness).
- metadata-only backup still contains manifest/version info.

## Registry
- Register this case in the E2E case registry (`wa-4vx.10.20`), including prerequisites and default inclusion status.

## Artifacts
- `backup.wa`, `backup_corrupt.wa`, `external.wa`
- `doctor_workspace_paths.txt` (workspace roots + db paths)
- `roundtrip_evidence.json` (stable-field evidence)
- `restore_verify.json` (verification results)
- `backup_restore_e2e.log` (timestamped steps + durations)

## Logging contract
- Every step logs:
  - scenario name
  - workspace path
  - command invoked (redacted)
  - pass/fail with durations
- On failure, print artifact pointers and a short summary.

## Testing
- Determinism:
  - no fixed sleeps; waits are explicit and bounded
- Safety:
  - scan all artifacts/logs for secret-like strings (reuse the redaction scan approach from `wa-4vx.10.18`)
  - ensure backups do not embed secrets in clear (especially config)
- Correctness:
  - verify round-trip evidence (token searchable; stable counts)
  - verify corruption rejection and no partial restore

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Round-trip export/import preserves core data.
- [ ] Corrupt backups are rejected with clear errors.
- [ ] Pre-import safety backup is created and verifiable.
- [ ] Metadata-only export exists and is smaller.
- [ ] Logs/artifacts are detailed, redacted, and sufficient to debug failures.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-18T19:56:30.14421545Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-02-11T00:47:48.304547-05:00","closed_at":"2026-01-29T03:08:49.571125579Z","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-v40a","depends_on_id":"wa-2vr1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"wa-v40a","depends_on_id":"wa-mxzp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T00:22:31Z","created_by":"import"}]}
